nativeRange.habitat: Edges of stagnant waters, rice paddies, and the banks of rivers, streams and rivulets across tropical and subtropical Asia. Naturalised and considered invasive in parts of the southeastern United States (Florida, Texas, Georgia), where it forms dense stands in canals and waterways.

decorPreferences: background placement in groups for fullest visual effect
soft to moderately hard water tolerated across a wide range
any standard aquarium substrate; nutrient-rich aquasoil promotes denser growth
balanced micronutrient and iron fertilisation for healthy green-to-reddish foliage

summary: An undemanding, fast-growing Asian stem plant with feathery, fine-cut foliage. Often recommended as an easier alternative to Cabomba, it tolerates a wide parameter range and is most striking when planted in dense background groups.
careNotes: Dwarf Ambulia (Limnophila sessiliflora) is a classic background stem plant native to tropical and subtropical Asia, belonging to the family Plantaginaceae. In the aquarium trade it is also sold as 'Ambulia' and 'Asian Marshweed', and is among the most widely traded Limnophila species in the hobby — often recommended as a more forgiving alternative to Cabomba. Related species in the trade include Limnophila aromatica (Rice Paddy Herb) and the trade cultivar Limnophila sp. 'Belem', both of which differ in care requirements and appearance. The plant grows best between 20 and 28 °C; it can tolerate cooler water but growth slows noticeably. Under aquarium conditions, stems typically reach 15 to 30 cm at moderate light and can extend to around 40 cm in taller tanks, with individual stems growing up to 3 to 4 cm wide. Growth is generally fast, and regular pruning is advisable to keep stands bushy — stems that receive weak light tend to become leggy and sparse. CO2 supplementation is not required but tends to produce more compact growth and a slight reddish tint at the shoot tips under stronger light; balanced micronutrients and iron support healthy green coloration throughout. Propagation is straightforward: trim the top shoot and replant it directly into the substrate, where it roots and branches readily. IMPORTANT REGULATORY NOTE: Limnophila sessiliflora is listed on the U.S. Federal Noxious Weed List (added 1981) and is regulated or prohibited in several states; it has naturalised in Florida, Texas, and Georgia, where it can form dense stands in canals and waterways. Aquarists in the United States should verify local legality before purchasing, never release plant fragments into wild waterways, and dispose of trimmings in household waste rather than down drains.
breedingNotes: Propagated by stem cuttings: trim the top 5-10 cm of an established stem and replant it directly into the substrate, where it roots and branches readily. Cutting the apical shoot also encourages lateral side branches on the mother stem, producing fuller bushes over time. Under good conditions the plant can also spread by runners.

primary.name: Tropica
primary.notes: Used for native origin (Asia / Southeast Asia), adult height (15-30+ cm, up to 40 cm), stem width (3-4 cm), light demand (low), CO2 demand (low — beneficial but not required), growth rate (fast), placement (background), propagation (cuttings, runners under good conditions), and difficulty (easy).

additional.name: Aquasabi
additional.notes: Cross-checked family (Plantaginaceae), native range (tropical Asia), height (15-60 cm reported), lighting (medium, ~0.5 W/L), CO2 (not necessary but beneficial), temperature (15-28 °C), pH (5-8), GH (0-30 °dGH), KH (2-21 °dKH), placement (background), propagation (top-shoot cuttings), difficulty (easy), and iron/micronutrient need.

additional.name: Flowgrow Aquatic Plant Database
additional.notes: Cross-checked family (Plantaginaceae), SE Asia origin, temperature optimum 20-26 °C (tolerance 15-28 °C), pH 5-8, KH 2-21, CO2 5-40 mg/L recommended for compact growth, fast growth, background placement, easy difficulty, and propagation by top-shoot cuttings.

additional.name: USDA APHIS Federal Noxious Weed List / Invasive.org
additional.notes: Used for U.S. regulatory status: Limnophila sessiliflora was added to the U.S. Federal Noxious Weed List in 1981 and is regulated by several states. Naturalised populations are documented in 22 Florida counties, 4 Texas counties, and 2 Georgia counties — supports the invasive-status note in careNotes.
